text: The surgeon begins the procedure by excising adhesions to allow exploration of the diaphragm. The falciform ligament is identified, and preparations are made for potential sectioning. The plan includes performing full-thickness excision of endometriotic lesions, with suturing to be done robotically using a grasp due to the unavailability of needle holders.

The surgeon identifies the falciform ligament amidst a dense area of adhesions near the diaphragm. The close proximity of the chest structures is noted, potentially complicating the surgical approach. The falciform ligament is visually confirmed and may be cut to enhance mobility during the procedure.

The surgeon identifies the falciform ligament and moves down to the peritoneum between the diaphragm and liver, noting the end of the liver. An endometriosis lesion with chocolate fluid is visible. The surgeon carefully dissects around the lesion, using a vacuum device cautiously to avoid bleeding, and considers asking the anesthesiologist to pause ventilation if diaphragm movements hinder the procedure.

The surgical field reveals numerous adhesions on the diaphragm, with both small and large endometriotic lesions visible. The surgeon prepares to resect the diaphragm entirely, noting the proximity of the posterior ramification of the phrenic nerve within a nodule. The hepatic vein is nearby but not in direct contact with the area being operated on. The surgeon emphasizes caution with the scissors to avoid unseen structures behind the diaphragm.

The surgeon begins by removing small lesions on the diaphragm without opening it to avoid pneumothorax. The peritoneum is opened, revealing that the diaphragm is more thickened laterally and less so at the phrenic center. The surgeon starts laterally, where opening the diaphragm is less common, and notes that the inflammation from these lesions irritates the phrenic nerve, causing referred pain to the right shoulder.

The surgeon approaches the diaphragm fibers, preparing to resect small lesions before opening the diaphragm. The diaphragm appears thickened, and the surgeon works outside the diaphragm to manage the lesions.

The surgeon is positioned laterally, working near the trocar for the bipolar instrument, and approaches large lesions in the thickened peritoneum. Most resectable lesions have been removed without opening the diaphragm.

The surgeon attempts to remove small lesions from the diaphragm without opening it, but the diaphragm becomes very thin, necessitating its opening. The lung becomes visible as the diaphragm is opened, and preparations are made to ensure enough tissue is available for suturing.

The surgical field shows a transfixion lesion on the diaphragm, requiring excision. The surgeon is preparing to suture the diaphragm, noting the absence of a needle driver which complicates the closure. The thoracic cavity is visible, and smoke from cauterization is present.

The surgeon adjusts the position of the robotic arms to improve the grasping function, ensuring the endoscope is not obstructed. The surgical team proceeds without stopping ventilation, using a 23-centimeter thread for suturing. The surgeon ties knots to secure the V-lock sutures, while managing the carbopneumoperitoneum by aspirating before closing the final knot.
